input_data.text_description: I provided a sketch. hand written, in 3d. it is clear. a simetrical design of a cabinet, empty spaces and all can be seen in the image. one thing to note is on top there is this steps design instead of a plaine line. Furthermore, the buldings that can be found on left and right have these two sides that are around 6cm (from left and right. the full building). this one that has a shape on the bottom and top on the cunt, you know hwat I am talking about. empty spaces and doors and all can be clearly seen in the image. the design should be light beiges painted wood.

images.generation_prompt:
Generate a PURE 2D ARCHITECTURAL FLOOR PLAN. This is a flat top-down diagram — like a printed AutoCAD plan view or a hand-drafted blueprint. NOT a 3D render. NOT a perspective view. NOT an isometric view. A flat 2D diagram with black lines on white paper, period.

Think of it as: you cut the building/object horizontally at mid-height and look straight down at the cross-section from directly above. The camera is mounted on the ceiling pointing straight down — zero tilt, zero angle.

WHAT TO DRAW

Generate a PURE 2D ARCHITECTURAL FRONT ELEVATION. This is a flat frontal diagram — like a printed AutoCAD elevation view or a hand-drafted architectural elevation. NOT a 3D render. NOT a perspective drawing. A flat 2D diagram with black lines on white paper showing the front face only.

Think of it as: you stand directly in front of the object at infinite distance and look straight at the face. The face appears as a perfectly flat rectangle. You see NO sides. NO top. NO depth. ONLY the flat fron

images.quality_feedback: Score: 6.4/10 | Strengths: Correct view type — top-down orthographic floor plan is appropriate for this furniture piece; Five primary vertical zones are correctly identified and labeled: Left Trim, Left Tower, Left Shelving Bay, Central Zone, Right Shelving Bay, Right Tower, Right Trim; The ~6cm outer trim strips are present on both left and right edges and are correctly labeled with the client-stated dimension annotation; Bilateral symmetry is reasonably maintained across the left-right axis; Continuous full-width plinth base is shown and labeled at the bottom; The projecting ledge is indicated in the central zone with a label, suggesting depth awareness; Door swing indicators are shown on the outer tower lower zones (left and right), correctly suggesting hinged doors; Clean line-weight drawing with clear labeling — professional CAD-style presentation; The overall width-to-depth proportion of the unit reads as a wide, shallow cabinet appropriate for a wall-placed unit | Issues: CRITICAL: The projecting ledge is shown only as a partial element in the central zone — it should span the full width of the central section (between the two outer towers), not just a small stub. The floor plan should show its full depth projection clearly across the entire central width; CRITICAL: The central zone does not differentiate between the upper double-door cabinet and the lower 2×3 drawer bank. In a floor plan, the drawer bank should show six drawer front lines (2 columns × 3 rows) and the lower flanking closed compartments should be distinct from the central drawer zone; CRITICAL: The lower flanking closed compartments (Lower Left and Lower Right Closed Compartments) are not shown as separate elements — they appear merged into the Left Shelving Bay and Right Shelving Bay zones respectively. These should be distinct base cabinet elements flanking the central drawer bank; The shelving bay horizontal lines appear in both upper and lower zones of the shelving bays, but a floor plan cut at a standard section height (approximately mid-height) should show a consistent single cut plane — the mixed representation of upper and lower shelf lines is ambiguous; The door swing arcs on the outer towers show 'GAP' labels which are unclear — these should be standard door swing arcs with no ambiguous gap notation; The projecting ledge depth is not clearly differentiated from the main carcass depth — the ledge should project forward (toward the viewer/room side) and this should be visible as a forward-projecting line in plan; No depth dimension or indication of the unit's front-to-back depth is provided — while dimensions are not required, the plan should at minimum show the carcass depth clearly through its outline; The central zone vertical division (separating left and right drawer columns) is not visible in the floor plan; The stepped crown profile — the primary design feature — is inherently a vertical/elevation feature and cannot be shown in floor plan, but no note or indication acknowledges this limitation; The right tower door swing appears to be missing or less clearly drawn compared to the left tower, slightly compromising symmetry in the lower zone | Suggestions: Redraw the central lower zone to clearly show: (a) the 2×3 drawer bank with central vertical division line, (b) the lower left closed compartment as a distinct bay to the left of the drawer bank, (c) the lower right closed compartment as a distinct bay to the right of the drawer bank — all three elements should be visible as separate plan zones; Extend the projecting ledge line to span the full width of the central section (from left inner bay partition to right inner bay partition) and show it as a forward-projecting element with visible depth — use a dashed line for the hidden back edge if needed; Add a note on the drawing stating: 'STEPPED CROWN PROFILE — SEE ELEVATION VIEW' to acknowledge that the primary design feature is not representable in plan; Clarify the door swing arcs on the outer tower lower doors — remove the 'GAP' label and replace with a proper quarter-circle door swing arc indicating the swing direction; Add a section cut line indicator showing where the horizontal cut plane is taken (typically at approximately 1000-1200mm above floor level for furniture plans); Show the projecting ledge as a distinct forward-projecting element — draw a second parallel line forward of the main carcass face line to indicate the ledge nose, with the ledge depth shown between the two lines; Ensure perfect bilateral symmetry in the door swing arcs — both left and right tower lower doors should have identical swing arc representations; Consider adding a thin dashed line to indicate the overhead extent of the upper cabinet zones (upper shelving, upper double-door cabinet) as is conventional in furniture floor plans; Label the lower flanking compartments explicitly as 'LOWER LEFT CABINET' and 'LOWER RIGHT CABINET' once they are correctly separated from the shelving bays; Add a north arrow or orientation indicator, or a note confirming this is a top-down view looking down from above

strengths: Correct view type — top-down orthographic floor plan is appropriate for this furniture piece
Five primary vertical zones are correctly identified and labeled: Left Trim, Left Tower, Left Shelving Bay, Central Zone, Right Shelving Bay, Right Tower, Right Trim
The ~6cm outer trim strips are present on both left and right edges and are correctly labeled with the client-stated dimension annotation
Bilateral symmetry is reasonably maintained across the left-right axis
Continuous full-width plinth base is shown and labeled at the bottom
The projecting ledge is indicated in the central zone with a label, suggesting depth awareness
Door swing indicators are shown on the outer tower lower zones (left and right), correctly suggesting hinged doors
Clean line-weight drawing with clear labeling — professional CAD-style presentation
The overall width-to-depth proportion of the unit reads as a wide, shallow cabinet appropriate for a wall-placed unit
issues: CRITICAL: The projecting ledge is shown only as a partial element in the central zone — it should span the full width of the central section (between the two outer towers), not just a small stub. The floor plan should show its full depth projection clearly across the entire central width

CRITICAL: The central zone does not differentiate between the upper double-door cabinet and the lower 2×3 drawer bank. In a floor plan, the drawer bank should show six drawer front lines (2 columns × 3 rows) and the lower flanking closed compartments should be distinct from the central drawer zone

CRITICAL: The lower flanking closed compartments (Lower Left and Lower Right Closed Compartments) are not shown as separate elements — they appear merged into the Left Shelving Bay and Right Shelving Bay zones respectively. These should be distinct base cabinet elements flanking the central drawer bank

The shelving bay horizontal lines appear in both upper and lower zones of the shelving bays, but a floor plan cut at a standard section height (approximately mid-height) should show a consistent single cut plane — the mixed representation of upper and lower shelf lines is ambiguous
The door swing arcs on the outer towers show 'GAP' labels which are unclear — these should be standard door swing arcs with no ambiguous gap notation

The projecting ledge depth is not clearly differentiated from the main carcass depth — the ledge should project forward (toward the viewer/room side) and this should be visible as a forward-projecting line in plan
No depth dimension or indication of the unit's front-to-back depth is provided — while dimensions are not required, the plan should at minimum show the carcass depth clearly through its outline
The central zone vertical division (separating left and right drawer columns) is not visible in the floor plan
The stepped crown profile — the primary design feature — is inherently a vertical/elevation feature and cannot be shown in floor plan, but no note or indication acknowledges this limitation
The right tower door swing appears to be missing or less clearly drawn compared to the left tower, slightly compromising symmetry in the lower zone
suggestions: Redraw the central lower zone to clearly show: (a) the 2×3 drawer bank with central vertical division line, (b) the lower left closed compartment as a distinct bay to the left of the drawer bank, (c) the lower right closed compartment as a distinct bay to the right of the drawer bank — all three elements should be visible as separate plan zones

Extend the projecting ledge line to span the full width of the central section (from left inner bay partition to right inner bay partition) and show it as a forward-projecting element with visible depth — use a dashed line for the hidden back edge if needed
Add a note on the drawing stating: 'STEPPED CROWN PROFILE — SEE ELEVATION VIEW' to acknowledge that the primary design feature is not representable in plan
Clarify the door swing arcs on the outer tower lower doors — remove the 'GAP' label and replace with a proper quarter-circle door swing arc indicating the swing direction
Add a section cut line indicator showing where the horizontal cut plane is taken (typically at approximately 1000-1200mm above floor level for furniture plans)

Show the projecting ledge as a distinct forward-projecting element — draw a second parallel line forward of the main carcass face line to indicate the ledge nose, with the ledge depth shown between the two lines
Ensure perfect bilateral symmetry in the door swing arcs — both left and right tower lower doors should have identical swing arc representations
Consider adding a thin dashed line to indicate the overhead extent of the upper cabinet zones (upper shelving, upper double-door cabinet) as is conventional in furniture floor plans
Label the lower flanking compartments explicitly as 'LOWER LEFT CABINET' and 'LOWER RIGHT CABINET' once they are correctly separated from the shelving bays
Add a north arrow or orientation indicator, or a note confirming this is a top-down view looking down from above

review_result.regeneration_instructions: FLOOR PLAN CORRECTIONS REQUIRED: (1) The central lower zone must be subdivided into THREE distinct elements: LEFT FLANKING BASE CABINET | CENTRAL 2×3 DRAWER BANK (with visible central vertical column divider) | RIGHT FLANKING BASE CABINET. These must not be merged into the shelving bays. (2) The projecting mid-level ledge must be drawn as a full-width forward-projecting surface spanning the entire central section width — show it as a double line (front edge + back edge) with visible depth projection toward the room/viewer side. (3) Door swing arcs on both outer tower lower doors must be identical quarter-circle arcs — remove 'GAP' labels and use standard architectural door swing notation. (4) The central zone must show the vertical division line between the two drawer columns. (5) Add a section cut line indicator. (6) Add a note: 'STEPPED CROWN PROFILE — SEE FRONT ELEVATION'. (7) Ensure all five zones plus two trim strips are correctly proportioned with strict bilateral symmetry. (8) The lower flanking compartments must be labeled separately from the inner shelving bays.

Think of it as: you cut the building/object horizontally at mid-height and look straight down at the cross-section from directly above. The camera is mounted on the ceiling pointing straight down — zero tilt, zero angle.

WHAT TO DRAW

CRITICAL: The mid-level projecting ledge is not clearly shown as a genuinely projecting counter surface with visible depth and underside — it reads more as a structural rail or flat line rather than a prominent projecting shelf spanning the full central width; The cornice detailing on the central section top appears relatively simple — the specification calls for a shaped molded cornice profile; The outer trim strips appear to have pilaster/column styling with fluting rather than the simpler cap block and plinth block profile specified | Suggestions: Render the outer trim strips as explicitly narrow (~6cm) flat vertical bands with simple square cap blocks at top and square plinth blocks at bottom — distinguish them clearly from the tower body; Show the mid-level projecting ledge with a visible shadow line and underside thickness — use a heavier line weight and show the ledge projecting forward of the cabinet face plane; Redraw the inner shelving bays with open compartments — remove panel framing from individual niches so they read as open display spaces, not closed cabinet doors; Clarify the lower flanking compartments to show one door panel per side only, not two; Increase the height differential of the stepped crown — the outer towers should be noticeably and dramatically taller than the central section to emphasize the stepped-crown as the primary design feature; Add a light beige fill tone or hatch notation to indicate the painted finish, or add a material callout note; Ensure the tower upper zones show open shelving with at least one fixed horizontal shelf creating two open niches — remove any door panel framing from these zones; Add more pronounced cornice molding profile at the top of the central section with a visible stepped/ogee profile; Verify symmetry is exact — left and right inner bays should have identical compartment grid layouts

strengths: Correct front elevation view type with proper label
Five-zone horizontal layout is clearly readable: outer towers, inner bays, central zone
Stepped crown silhouette is present — outer towers are taller than center, stepping down toward middle
Central upper zone correctly shows double-door closed cabinet with two plain panel doors meeting at center
Six drawers in 2×3 arrangement (two columns, three rows) with central vertical division are correctly rendered
Lower section correctly shows outer tower doors, flanking closed compartments, and central drawer bank
Continuous full-width plinth base is present with no legs
Inner open display shelving bays show multi-compartment bookcase grid arrangement
Bilateral symmetry is largely maintained across the composition
Mid-level horizontal division between upper and lower sections is visible
Drawer pulls are shown on each drawer front
Cornice/crown molding detail is present at the top of the central section
Professional line-drawing quality appropriate for architectural elevation
issues: CRITICAL: The outer decorative trim strips (~6cm wide) are not clearly rendered as distinct narrow vertical elements separate from the tower sides — they appear merged into the tower pilaster/column detailing rather than being explicit standalone trim strips with shaped cap block at top and plinth block at bottom

CRITICAL: The mid-level projecting ledge is not clearly shown as a genuinely projecting counter surface with visible depth and underside — it reads more as a structural rail or flat line rather than a prominent projecting shelf spanning the full central width

The inner shelving bays appear to show panel-framed closed compartments rather than fully open-fronted display shelving — the compartments have visible panel borders suggesting closed doors rather than open niches
The lower flanking compartments appear to show TWO door panels each (left and right of center) rather than one door per side — this adds extra door panels not specified
The stepped crown profile, while present, is not dramatically pronounced enough — the height differential between outer towers and central section could be more significant
The image is rendered in white/line drawing only — the specification calls for light beige painted finish which should be indicated even in elevation (via tone/fill or notation)
The outer tower upper zones appear to show framed panel sections rather than clearly open shelving niches — open shelving should read as open compartments without door panel framing
The cornice detailing on the central section top appears relatively simple — the specification calls for a shaped molded cornice profile
The outer trim strips appear to have pilaster/column styling with fluting rather than the simpler cap block and plinth block profile specified
suggestions: Render the outer trim strips as explicitly narrow (~6cm) flat vertical bands with simple square cap blocks at top and square plinth blocks at bottom — distinguish them clearly from the tower body
Show the mid-level projecting ledge with a visible shadow line and underside thickness — use a heavier line weight and show the ledge projecting forward of the cabinet face plane
Redraw the inner shelving bays with open compartments — remove panel framing from individual niches so they read as open display spaces, not closed cabinet doors
Clarify the lower flanking compartments to show one door panel per side only, not two

Increase the height differential of the stepped crown — the outer towers should be noticeably and dramatically taller than the central section to emphasize the stepped-crown as the primary design feature
Add a light beige fill tone or hatch notation to indicate the painted finish, or add a material callout note
Ensure the tower upper zones show open shelving with at least one fixed horizontal shelf creating two open niches — remove any door panel framing from these zones
Add more pronounced cornice molding profile at the top of the central section with a visible stepped/ogee profile
Verify symmetry is exact — left and right inner bays should have identical compartment grid layouts

review_result.regeneration_instructions: CORRECTIONS REQUIRED FOR REGENERATION: (1) OUTER TRIM STRIPS: Render the ~6cm outer trim strips as explicitly distinct narrow flat vertical bands at the extreme left and right edges, clearly separate from the tower body. Top end: simple square cap block. Bottom end: simple square plinth block. Do NOT render as pilasters or fluted columns. (2) MID-LEVEL PROJECTING LEDGE: The horizontal ledge separating upper and lower central sections MUST show visible projection depth — draw with a thick shadow line below and show the underside face of the ledge. It must read as a genuine counter surface projecting forward, not a flat rail. (3) INNER SHELVING BAYS: Remove all door panel framing from individual compartments in the inner bays — these are OPEN shelving niches. Compartments should read as open rectangular voids, not framed panel doors. (4) TOWER UPPER ZONES: Remove any door panel framing from the tower upper zones — these are OPEN shelving niches with one fixed shelf creating two open compartments. (5) LOWER FLANKING COMPARTMENTS: Show exactly ONE hinged door panel per flanking compartment (one left of drawer bank, one right of drawer bank). (6) STEPPED CROWN HEIGHT: Increase the height differential between outer towers and central section — the step down should be dramatic and clearly the primary silhouette feature. (7) LIGHT BEIGE FILL: Apply a uniform light beige tone fill to all cabinet surfaces to indicate the painted finish. (8) CORNICE MOLDING: Add a more pronounced shaped cornice profile at the top of the central section with visible molding steps.

The crown appears as a relatively flat or minimally differentiated top profile. The specification demands a dramatic staircase stepped silhouette where outer towers are visibly and significantly taller than the center. This is the PRIMARY design feature and it is largely absent.; CRITICAL — Outer decorative trim strips (~6cm wide) are not clearly identifiable as distinct narrow applied elements at the extreme left and right edges with shaped profiled detailing at top and bottom. They appear merged into the tower side returns.; The outer towers appear to have a cornice/crown treatment at their tops but the step-down toward the center is not dramatic enough — the height differential between towers and central section is insufficient.; The lower flanking compartments between the drawer bank and the outer towers are rendered as open shelving bays rather than closed-front cabinet elements (hinged door panels) as specified.; The inner shelving bays in the lower section below the mid-level ledge appear open rather than closed, which may be acceptable but the lower flanking compartments specifically should be closed doors.; Symmetry is slightly imperfect — the left tower appears partially cut off by the window/wall edge, reducing the visual symmetry of the composition in the render. | Suggestions: Dramatically increase the height differential of the outer towers relative to the central section — the towers should step up at least 25-35% taller than the central cabinet top, creating a clear staircase silhouette; Add distinct narrow (~6cm) vertical trim strips at the extreme left and right edges with square cap blocks at top and plinth blocks at bottom as separate applied elements; Paint the mid-level projecting ledge/counter surface in the same light beige as the rest of the unit — remove any wood-tone or contrasting finish from this surface; Replace the open lower shelving flanking the drawer bank with closed door panels (hinged base cabinet doors) on both left and right sides of the drawer bank; Ensure the outer tower upper zones each show two distinct open niches created by at least one fixed horizontal shelf; strengths: Excellent photorealistic render quality with convincing lighting, shadows, and real-world context (room, sofa, rug, props)
Light beige painted wood finish is well-executed and consistent across the unit
Central double-door closed cabinet is correctly rendered with two plain tall door panels meeting at center
Six drawers in 2 columns × 3 rows are correctly shown with individual pulls — this critical requirement is met
Mid-level projecting ledge is rendered as a genuine projecting surface with visible depth and underside — correctly executed
Continuous full-width plinth base is present with no legs
Inner open display shelving bays are present on both sides with multi-compartment bookcase grid arrangements
Overall bilateral symmetry is largely maintained
Contemporary transitional with classical millwork character reads clearly
View type is correct — photorealistic render in a real-world residential interior environment
issues: CRITICAL — Stepped crown profile is NOT correctly executed: The outer towers do not rise significantly higher than the central section. The crown appears as a relatively flat or minimally differentiated top profile. The specification demands a dramatic staircase stepped silhouette where outer towers are visibly and significantly taller than the center. This is the PRIMARY design feature and it is largely absent.

CRITICAL — Outer decorative trim strips (~6cm wide) are not clearly identifiable as distinct narrow applied elements at the extreme left and right edges with shaped profiled detailing at top and bottom. They appear merged into the tower side returns.

The outer towers appear to have a cornice/crown treatment at their tops but the step-down toward the center is not dramatic enough — the height differential between towers and central section is insufficient.

The lower section of the outer towers shows a single closed door panel, which is correct, but the upper zone of the towers appears to show only one shelf niche rather than two open niches created by at least one fixed shelf — the upper tower shelving configuration is ambiguous.

The mid-level ledge/counter surface appears to show a wood-tone (lighter, natural wood color) rather than the specified uniform light beige painted finish — this introduces a secondary color that violates the single-color specification.
The lower flanking compartments between the drawer bank and the outer towers are rendered as open shelving bays rather than closed-front cabinet elements (hinged door panels) as specified.
The inner shelving bays in the lower section below the mid-level ledge appear open rather than closed, which may be acceptable but the lower flanking compartments specifically should be closed doors.
Symmetry is slightly imperfect — the left tower appears partially cut off by the window/wall edge, reducing the visual symmetry of the composition in the render.
suggestions: Dramatically increase the height differential of the outer towers relative to the central section — the towers should step up at least 25-35% taller than the central cabinet top, creating a clear staircase silhouette
Add distinct narrow (~6cm) vertical trim strips at the extreme left and right edges with square cap blocks at top and plinth blocks at bottom as separate applied elements
Paint the mid-level projecting ledge/counter surface in the same light beige as the rest of the unit — remove any wood-tone or contrasting finish from this surface
Replace the open lower shelving flanking the drawer bank with closed door panels (hinged base cabinet doors) on both left and right sides of the drawer bank
Ensure the outer tower upper zones each show two distinct open niches created by at least one fixed horizontal shelf
Reframe the render to show the full unit symmetrically centered in the frame without architectural elements (windows, walls) cropping either side
Strengthen the step transitions at the crown to be clean right-angle geometry — add a horizontal fascia board at each step level

review_result.regeneration_instructions: CRITICAL FIXES REQUIRED: (1) STEPPED CROWN MUST BE DRAMATIC — outer towers must be visibly 30-40% taller than the central section, with clean right-angle step-down transitions forming a clear staircase silhouette. This is the primary design feature. (2) Add distinct narrow ~6cm decorative trim strips at the extreme left and right outer edges of the unit as separate applied vertical elements with square cap block detailing at top and square plinth block at bottom. (3) The mid-level projecting ledge/counter surface must be painted the same uniform light beige as the entire unit — NO wood grain or contrasting wood tone on this surface. (4) The lower flanking compartments on each side of the central drawer bank must be CLOSED DOOR PANELS (hinged base cabinet doors), not open shelving. (5) Frame the render to show the complete unit symmetrically centered without any architectural elements cropping the left or right sides. (6) Maintain all correctly rendered elements: 6 drawers in 2×3 grid, central double-door cabinet, open inner shelving bays, continuous plinth base, light beige painted finish throughout.

images.quality_feedback: Score: 7.0/10 | Strengths: Correct orthographic top-down floor plan view type is used with proper drafting conventions; All five primary vertical zones are present and correctly labeled: left outer tower, left open shelving bay, central zone, right open shelving bay, right outer tower; Left and right outer decorative trim strips (~6cm) are shown and labeled at both top and bottom of the drawing — a critical client-stated dimension correctly honored; Bilateral symmetry is largely maintained across the composition; Central 2x3 drawer bank is correctly shown with two columns and three rows, with pull marks visible; Lower left and lower right closed compartments are shown with door swing arcs indicating hinged doors — correct functional interpretation; Tower doors are shown with door swing arcs in both outer towers — correct; Projecting ledge is labeled and indicated as a separate depth element; Plinth base is shown as a continuous full-width element at the bottom; Section cut reference markers (A-A') are included — professional drafting practice; Upper double doors are labeled and shown with a central meeting line; Note referencing stepped crown profile to front elevation is a good cross-reference annotation; Overall layout proportions (wider than tall) are consistent with the specification of a substantially wide unit | Issues: CRITICAL: The upper double-door central cabinet shows a large semicircular arch element (fanlight/lunette) on the door faces — this is NOT specified and directly contradicts the instruction that door fronts are 'plain, uninterrupted panel faces.' This decorative arch must be removed entirely.; CRITICAL: In a floor plan (top-down view), door swing arcs for the upper double-door cabinet should not show an arch profile — the arc shown appears to be a decorative elevation element incorrectly placed in plan view. Door swings in plan should be simple quarter-circle arcs.; The upper shelving bays (left and right inner open display bays) show horizontal shelf lines in plan view — while technically acceptable as a representation convention, the depth differentiation between open shelving and closed cabinet zones is not clearly distinguished in plan.; The projecting ledge is labeled but its projection depth relative to the carcass face is not clearly differentiated — the ledge should show a distinct forward projection line indicating it protrudes beyond the main cabinet face depth.; The outer towers in plan view do not clearly show the depth difference or separation from the inner bays — the internal partition lines between tower and inner bay could be more clearly delineated.; Material hatching or fill is absent — a floor plan of this type would benefit from light hatching on cut sections (walls/panels) to distinguish solid elements from open/void spaces, improving readability.; The wall behind the unit (shown as a thick border) implies the unit is built-in or recessed into a wall — the specification states freestanding or wall-placed, so the rear wall representation may be misleading without clarification.; The section cut A-A' markers appear at both top and bottom but the cut line itself is not clearly drawn through the plan — the cut plane location should be more explicitly indicated.; No depth dimension is provided for the unit — while the specification notes only the 6cm trim strip width is confirmed, a floor plan without any depth reference is incomplete for fabrication purposes.; The lower flanking closed compartments show door swings that appear to open outward toward the front — the swing direction and clearance should be verified for consistency with the projecting ledge above. | Suggestions: IMMEDIATELY REMOVE the semicircular arch/fanlight element from the central upper double-door cabinet representation — replace with a simple plan view showing two door panels with quarter-circle swing arcs opening left and right respectively, with a straight central meeting line.; Add light section hatching (diagonal lines at 45°) to all solid panel/carcass elements that are cut by the plan section plane — this is standard architectural drafting practice and dramatically improves readability.; Show the projecting ledge with a clear offset line indicating its forward projection beyond the main carcass face depth — use a dashed line or a distinct second contour line to indicate the ledge overhang.; Add a depth dimension reference (even if marked TBC or approximate) to indicate the overall front-to-back depth of the unit — suggest approximately 350-400mm for the main carcass and 450-500mm for the tower zones.; Clarify the rear wall representation — if the unit is freestanding, the thick border should be replaced with a simple back panel line; if wall-placed, add a note indicating 'existing wall beyond.'; Ensure the section cut line A-A' is drawn as a dashed centerline through the plan with clear arrowheads indicating the viewing direction for the corresponding elevation view.; Add a scale bar or noted scale (e.g., 1:20 or 1:25) to the floor plan — currently no scale reference is present, which is a significant omission for a technical drawing.; Consider adding depth labels for key zones (e.g., 'Tower depth,' 'Shelving bay depth,' 'Drawer bank depth') to communicate any depth variations between modules.; Verify that the lower compartment door swings do not conflict with the projecting ledge above — if the ledge projects significantly, the door swing radius may need to be reduced or the doors may need to be shown as sliding or bifold.; Add a north arrow or orientation indicator if this is intended as a room-context floor plan, or remove the surrounding wall border entirely if the intent is a furniture plan only.

strengths: Correct orthographic top-down floor plan view type is used with proper drafting conventions
All five primary vertical zones are present and correctly labeled: left outer tower, left open shelving bay, central zone, right open shelving bay, right outer tower
Left and right outer decorative trim strips (~6cm) are shown and labeled at both top and bottom of the drawing — a critical client-stated dimension correctly honored
Bilateral symmetry is largely maintained across the composition
Central 2x3 drawer bank is correctly shown with two columns and three rows, with pull marks visible
Lower left and lower right closed compartments are shown with door swing arcs indicating hinged doors — correct functional interpretation
Tower doors are shown with door swing arcs in both outer towers — correct
Projecting ledge is labeled and indicated as a separate depth element
Plinth base is shown as a continuous full-width element at the bottom
Section cut reference markers (A-A') are included — professional drafting practice
Upper double doors are labeled and shown with a central meeting line
Note referencing stepped crown profile to front elevation is a good cross-reference annotation
Overall layout proportions (wider than tall) are consistent with the specification of a substantially wide unit
issues: CRITICAL: The upper double-door central cabinet shows a large semicircular arch element (fanlight/lunette) on the door faces — this is NOT specified and directly contradicts the instruction that door fronts are 'plain, uninterrupted panel faces.' This decorative arch must be removed entirely.

CRITICAL: In a floor plan (top-down view), door swing arcs for the upper double-door cabinet should not show an arch profile — the arc shown appears to be a decorative elevation element incorrectly placed in plan view. Door swings in plan should be simple quarter-circle arcs.

The upper shelving bays (left and right inner open display bays) show horizontal shelf lines in plan view — while technically acceptable as a representation convention, the depth differentiation between open shelving and closed cabinet zones is not clearly distinguished in plan.

The projecting ledge is labeled but its projection depth relative to the carcass face is not clearly differentiated — the ledge should show a distinct forward projection line indicating it protrudes beyond the main cabinet face depth.
The outer towers in plan view do not clearly show the depth difference or separation from the inner bays — the internal partition lines between tower and inner bay could be more clearly delineated.

Material hatching or fill is absent — a floor plan of this type would benefit from light hatching on cut sections (walls/panels) to distinguish solid elements from open/void spaces, improving readability.

The wall behind the unit (shown as a thick border) implies the unit is built-in or recessed into a wall — the specification states freestanding or wall-placed, so the rear wall representation may be misleading without clarification.
The section cut A-A' markers appear at both top and bottom but the cut line itself is not clearly drawn through the plan — the cut plane location should be more explicitly indicated.

No depth dimension is provided for the unit — while the specification notes only the 6cm trim strip width is confirmed, a floor plan without any depth reference is incomplete for fabrication purposes.

The lower flanking closed compartments show door swings that appear to open outward toward the front — the swing direction and clearance should be verified for consistency with the projecting ledge above.
suggestions: IMMEDIATELY REMOVE the semicircular arch/fanlight element from the central upper double-door cabinet representation — replace with a simple plan view showing two door panels with quarter-circle swing arcs opening left and right respectively, with a straight central meeting line.

Add light section hatching (diagonal lines at 45°) to all solid panel/carcass elements that are cut by the plan section plane — this is standard architectural drafting practice and dramatically improves readability.

Show the projecting ledge with a clear offset line indicating its forward projection beyond the main carcass face depth — use a dashed line or a distinct second contour line to indicate the ledge overhang.

Add a depth dimension reference (even if marked TBC or approximate) to indicate the overall front-to-back depth of the unit — suggest approximately 350-400mm for the main carcass and 450-500mm for the tower zones.
Clarify the rear wall representation — if the unit is freestanding, the thick border should be replaced with a simple back panel line; if wall-placed, add a note indicating 'existing wall beyond.'
Ensure the section cut line A-A' is drawn as a dashed centerline through the plan with clear arrowheads indicating the viewing direction for the corresponding elevation view.
Add a scale bar or noted scale (e.g., 1:20 or 1:25) to the floor plan — currently no scale reference is present, which is a significant omission for a technical drawing.
Consider adding depth labels for key zones (e.g., 'Tower depth,' 'Shelving bay depth,' 'Drawer bank depth') to communicate any depth variations between modules.

Verify that the lower compartment door swings do not conflict with the projecting ledge above — if the ledge projects significantly, the door swing radius may need to be reduced or the doors may need to be shown as sliding or bifold.
Add a north arrow or orientation indicator if this is intended as a room-context floor plan, or remove the surrounding wall border entirely if the intent is a furniture plan only.

images.generation_prompt:
Generate a PURE 2D ARCHITECTURAL FLOOR PLAN. This is a flat top-down diagram — like a printed AutoCAD plan view or a hand-drafted blueprint. NOT a 3D render. NOT a perspective view. NOT an isometric view. A flat 2D diagram with black lines on white paper, period.

Think of it as: you cut the building/object horizontally at mid-height and look straight down at the cross-section from directly above. The camera is mounted on the ceiling pointing straight down — zero tilt, zero angle.

WHAT TO DRAW
